summary: The main claim is: Research Institutional Design for Evaluating Alignment Plans 1.
Plan for How to Evaluate Automated Alignment Researchers 1.
The main claim is: The map is populated bythis spreadsheet, so if you have corrections or suggestions please leave a comment.
There's also a google form and a Discord channel for suggestions.
The main claim is: It is a go karting competition between three factions: AI capabilities researchers, AI existential safety researchers, and AI bias and ethics researchers.
If bias and ethics win: + Every capabilities researcher will have a bias and ethics expert sit behind them while they work.
The main claim is: Some of these researchers have therefore branched out into the novel subfield of AI Safety.
An AI should in fact be expected to resist any such attempt, since goal modification would endanger carrying out its current one.
The main claim is: In a previous post, I argued that the answer is "singularities" — minimum loss points with ill-defined tangents.
What explains the "spooky action at a distance" of singularities in the loss landscape?
The main claim is: There are quite a lot of people who aren’t in the AI safety community and have a stake in this, so maybe some of them could do something.
I think when people say ‘we’ here, they generally imagine that they are strategizing on behalf of, a) the AI safety community, b) the USA, c) themselves or d) they and their readers.
The main claim is: Next steps ---------- Now, what can we do to measure and facilitate progress on alignment and AI safety?
The main claim is: Random Pruning -------------- What does random pruning do to a network.
If you take the untrained network, and train it, the result looks like this.

The main claim is: When an AI hits situations its training didn't cover (e.g. trained on adults, then it meets a child), we want it to notice the ambiguity, reinterpret its goal, and ask a human when multiple goals fit the data.
Aligned AI's new benchmark operationalizes this for image classifiers, measuring how well an algorithm detects ambiguous inputs and avoids goal misgeneralization via a single active-learning query.
The main claim is: I’ve seen people argue that AGI will never exist, and even if we can get an AI to do everything a human can do, that won’t be “true” general intelligence.

“If the AI can replace more than half of all jobs humans can currently do, then it is definitely an AGI—as an average human can do an average number of jobs after a finite training period, it should be no different for an Artificial Genera…
